user_prompt: Prove that the sum of two odd integers is even.
trace:
<steer>restate the claim in algebraic form</steer>
<exec>Write the two odd integers as 2a+1 and 2b+1 for integers a and b. Then compute their sum and simplify.</exec>

<steer>carry out the simplification</steer>
<exec>(2a+1)+(2b+1)=2a+2b+2=2(a+b+1), so the sum is divisible by 2.</exec>

<steer>connect divisibility by 2 to evenness</steer>
<exec>An integer is even exactly when it can be written as 2 times another integer. Since a+b+1 is an integer, the sum is even.</exec>

<steer>state the proof cleanly</steer>
<exec>This completes the proof because every step used the defining form of odd integers and ended with the defining form of an even integer.</exec>
answer: The sum of two odd integers is even.

user_prompt: Given a nested loop implementation, decide whether the runtime is O(n^2) or better.
trace:
<steer>identify what the loops actually iterate over</steer>
<exec>The outer loop runs n times, but the inner loop does not always run n times. Its bound depends on the shrinking remainder of the input.</exec>

<steer>write the total work as a sum</steer>
<exec>Instead of multiplying n by n immediately, express the total number of inner iterations as a series so the structure is visible.</exec>

<steer>estimate the series</steer>
<exec>If the inner work shrinks geometrically or by a harmonic pattern, the total can be below n squared. The right answer depends on the exact sum, not the visual presence of nesting.</exec>

<steer>decide which asymptotic family fits</steer>
<exec>Once the sum is simplified, compare it to standard growth rates like n log n and n squared rather than relying on the syntax of the code alone.</exec>
answer: The runtime depends on the summed inner work, not just the fact that loops are nested.

user_prompt: Choose between launching a narrow beta now or waiting for a broader release with more polish.
trace:
<steer>state the decision in terms of tradeoffs</steer>
<exec>The choice is between learning earlier with a smaller surface area and reducing reputational risk with a more polished but later release.</exec>

<steer>identify the main decision criteria</steer>
<exec>The key criteria are speed of learning, user trust, engineering bandwidth, and how costly it is to reverse mistakes after launch.</exec>

<steer>compare the two options on those criteria</steer>
<exec>A narrow beta scores better on fast feedback and bounded risk, while a later broader release may score better on polish but delays real-world signal.</exec>

<steer>infer what would tip the decision</steer>
<exec>If the team can tightly constrain the beta population and respond quickly, the narrow beta becomes more attractive. If failure would be highly public and sticky, waiting gains weight.</exec>
answer: The better choice depends on whether feedback speed or public-risk control matters more under the current constraints.
